facts.text: Port Washington was settled in 1644 when 18 English families bought land from the Matinecock Indians and named it “Cow Neck.”
In the 1870s, Port Washington became a major sand‑mining town; its sandbank was the largest east of the Mississippi and supplied concrete for the Empire State and Chrysler buildings.
The John Philip Sousa Memorial Bandshell, dedicated in 1967, sits in Sunset Park and honors the composer who once lived nearby.
The Long Island Rail Road extended its Port Washington Branch in 1898, opening the Manhasset and Port Washington stations and spurring rapid growth.
Port Washington’s waterfront hosts Harbor Links, a golf course built on former sand mines that opened in 1998.
